entries.summary: Helper return dereferenced without a nil check
entries.common_cause: The verifier rejects a load through a pointer whose nullability has not been proven. This typically means a helper return (bpf_map_lookup_elem, bpf_ringbuf_reserve, bpf_sk_lookup_tcp, etc.) was dereferenced before a nil check on every path. The verifier tracks the helper return as a scalar until it is compared against NULL.
entries.remediation: Insert a nil guard ({{if .Captures.register}}for {{.Captures.register}}{{end}}) before dereferencing the helper's return: in .hzn, use a `match` arm or `if x != nil { ... }` and reference the checked local. If the Horizon validator did not flag this in source, the alias graph (Phase 1 #1) missed the binding — file a bug.

entries.summary: Scalar value used where pointer is expected
entries.common_cause: The verifier saw a register with type 'inv' (an inferred scalar of unknown provenance) used in a memory access. This usually means a value was synthesized through arithmetic that lost its pointer type, or an uninitialized register was dereferenced. Unlike VC0001, the verifier did not even see a candidate pointer here.
entries.remediation: Avoid raw pointer arithmetic in .hzn. Use compiler-known helpers (packet helpers, map accessors) that preserve verifier-safe types, and ensure every value used as a pointer is bound from a typed helper return.

entries.summary: Out-of-bounds access on map value, packet, or stack pointer

patterns: min_value is outside of the (?:array range|allowed memory range)

entries.common_cause: The verifier proved an access could exceed the bounds of the underlying region — a map_value, packet payload, or stack frame. The offset or index was either a runtime scalar with no upper bound the verifier could prove, or a constant that already exceeds the region's size.
entries.remediation: Use Horizon packet helpers (`bpf.packet.read_*`) and bounded index operations so the verifier can prove the access stays in-region. For map values, prefer typed accessors that carry size proofs; for stack data, keep the index a literal or a value derived from a verifier-known bound.

entries.summary: Unreleased reference (ringbuf reserve or sk_lookup leak)
entries.common_cause: The verifier tracks reference-counted helper returns (bpf_ringbuf_reserve, bpf_sk_lookup_tcp, bpf_skc_lookup_tcp) and rejects programs that return without calling the matching release helper on every control-flow path. The leaked reference id in the message identifies which acquisition was not paired.
entries.remediation: Submit or discard every ringbuf reservation (`bpf.ringbuf.submit` / `bpf.ringbuf.discard`) on all return paths; release every `bpf_sk_lookup_tcp` result with `bpf_sk_release`. In .hzn, prefer the scoped-resource helpers that release automatically on scope exit.

entries.summary: Unbounded loop (no upper bound the verifier can prove)
entries.common_cause: The verifier rejected a back-edge in the control-flow graph because it could not prove the loop terminates within the insn budget. Either the loop bound is a runtime scalar with no known upper bound, or the loop variable is mutated in a way that breaks the verifier's monotonicity proof.
entries.remediation: Use a counted `for` loop with a literal or integer-constant upper bound, or wrap iteration in `bpf_loop` with an explicit max-iterations argument. Keep the loop variable monotonic in a verifier-friendly way (`i = i + 1`, never branch-dependent mutations).

entries.summary: BPF program exceeds verifier instruction budget

patterns: BPF program is too large\. Processed \d+ insn

entries.common_cause: The verifier walks all reachable paths in the program and gives up after one million insns (the kernel default). The program either has too much straight-line code, too many branches expanding the path count, or a loop that the verifier is unrolling instead of bounding via bpf_loop.
entries.remediation: Factor the program into smaller pieces using tail-calls or `bpf_loop` to fold repeated work into a bounded iteration. Avoid macro-expanded unrolled loops; prefer explicit bounded loops the verifier can analyze structurally.

entries.summary: Helper not available for this program type

patterns: program of this type cannot use helper bpf_\w+
helper call is not allowed in probe

entries.common_cause: Each BPF program type has an allowlist of helpers; the verifier rejects calls outside that allowlist. {{if .Captures.helper}}The offending helper is bpf_{{.Captures.helper}}.{{end}} The most common cause is calling a tracing-only helper from a network program or vice versa.
entries.remediation: Use only Horizon compiler-known helpers for this program kind. If you need a helper that is technically available but not yet wrapped, add a typed helper wrapper to the Horizon stdlib before calling it from .hzn so the validator catches the kind mismatch up front.

entries.summary: Uninitialized return value (R0 not set on all paths)
entries.common_cause: The verifier reached the end of the program (or a function) on a control-flow path that did not assign R0, the BPF return-value register. Every return path must produce an explicit value — falling off the end is not allowed.
entries.remediation: Return an explicit i32 action or value on every control-flow path. In .hzn, use `return` with a value (or an exhaustive `match` that yields one) at the end of every branch.

entries.summary: Stack overflow (combined frame size exceeds BPF stack limit)

patterns: combined stack size of \d+ calls is \d+\. Too large

entries.common_cause: BPF stack frames are limited to 512 bytes total (across all called functions in a tail-call-free call chain). The verifier rejects programs whose summed stack usage exceeds this limit. The cause is usually a large local struct or array that should live in a map or ringbuf reservation instead.
entries.remediation: Move large local records into maps (`BPF_MAP_TYPE_PERCPU_ARRAY` keyed by 0 is the canonical scratch-pad) or ringbuf reservations. Avoid recursive helpers; prefer flat call graphs so the verifier's combined-stack accounting stays bounded.

entries.summary: Misaligned memory access on stack, packet, or value pointer
entries.common_cause: On architectures that require natural alignment (and on architectures where the kernel was built with `CONFIG_EFFICIENT_UNALIGNED_ACCESS=n`), the verifier rejects loads or stores whose effective address is not aligned to the access width. The verifier sees this statically from the offset and the access size.
entries.remediation: Use the access width that matches the field's natural alignment (load a u32 from a 4-byte-aligned offset, etc.). For packet data, prefer Horizon packet helpers that perform aligned reads with explicit width arguments.
